Description:
In small groups (3-4), students respond to a prompt on a shared Google slide (see template in resources) and then do a short presentation to teach the class what they have learned - similar to a jigsaw.

Sample prompts for different groups for . . .Science
- Describe the first phase of photosynthesis. 
- Describe the second phase of photosynthesis?
- What parts of the cell are involved in photosynthesis?
- What is ATP?  Explain the parts.

Sample prompts for different groups for . . .Social Studies 
- List some reasons why countries go to war.  Explain each one.
- What was the political and economic situation in Germany before WWII?
- What sparked WWII and why?
- Describe the alliances formed between countries in WWII.
- Describe the weaponry and tactics used in WWII.

Description:
Updating Notes takes minimal preparation and it is a great way to use the 
Here’s how to do it:
- Lecture for 10 minutes
- Give students 2 minutes to share and compare their notes with a partner (collaboration).
- Give students 2 minutes to make revisions in their notes.

This technique allows students to take a break from listening, reflect, interact with peers, fill in gaps, develop joint questions as well as encouraging them to create better notes.

Description:
Practicing gratitude has powerful benefits for students including better mental health, greater resilience, stronger social connections, stress reduction, and a more positive mindset about the future.
A Gratitude Journal is a personal reflection (ungraded) with brief entries and optional sharing with peers or the whole class.
This article lists gratitude prompts for 15 days, but this can be shortened as needed.  Each week has a theme . . .
- Week 1: Reflecting on Our School Year\
- Week 2: Appreciating Our Present
- Week 3: Looking Forward with Gratitude
It also has activity ideas such as a gratitude circle, gratitude wall, thank-you letter writing, gratitude quotes, and even planting a gratitude garden. 
Students can continue this journal over the summer to benefit and grow even more
Resource Links: End the Year with Gratitude: A 15-Day Journal Practice for Students

Description:
Today’s students crave constant stimulation and entertainment. Ideas to draw them in include -  Read the article for examples.
1. Surprise Them - Add novelty or something unusual/unexpected to arouse curiosity 😲.  Could be a song, odd clothes/accessory.
2. Present Odd Facts, Anomalies - Give students info that contradicts their intuition or a live demo that defies logic 🤯.
3. Invite Student Predictions - Students guess what will happen and eagerly anticipate the “Big Reveal” 😅.
4. Pose a Provocative (Hook) ? - Seek out a burning, open-ended question related to your lesson content. Students will 🤔.
5. Cite a Current Event/Issue - Raise awareness of breaking news 📰 relevant to them. Ask for their reaction/opinion.
6. Be Humorous - Use a funny joke 😂, meme, cartoon, comic strip, song, or video to give students a shot of dopamine and segue into your lesson with momentum.

Description:
Students thrive and engagement soars when they are given a choice of what product to create to demonstrate their learning.  But what choices should you offer, what tools are available, and what rubric should you use?

Check out this “Make a Choice Board” document that will walk you through the process AND provide examples of choice boards that you can copy and modify. It even has rubrics!  More choice board options.

Tips for Choice Board Success
- Start small - too many choices can be overwhelming for students.  Start with 3.
- Set time limits - use a timer to keep students progressing through the process such as picking a partner (1 min), picking a topic/product (2 min), finishing a rough draft (30 min), self-grade/reflect (10 min)
- Constant feedback - Regularly circulate the room to check that students’ products align with the rubric requirements.  Comment on their work frequently.
Resource Links:
Choice Boards: Benefits, Design Tips, and Differentiation
Using Choice Boards for Students to Differentiate Instruction

Description:
Students who interact, share, and learn from each other build a community of learners in the classroom that develops their self-efficacy and communication skills.  Try these techniques to slowly get them there - 
- Quiz, Quiz, Trade - Give each student a question.  Stand up, hands up -- students pair up with someone with a hand up.  Partners quiz each other, switch ?s, repeat the process with hands up to pair up again.
- Opinion Poll - Give each student a poll question and have them ask the ? to as many students as possible in 7 min.  Then, ask students to briefly summarize their results.
- Brainwriting - Students write down their ideas about a ? or topic for 2-3 min. Each student passes their paper to another student to read and add new ideas.  Repeat this until they get their original paper back.  
Learn about more techniques by clicking on the resource.
Resource Links: Class Discussion Techniques Explained

Description:
Students can use concept maps (WICOR) to organize info into a visual to make stronger mental connections between concepts than traditional notes. 
Follow these steps to utilize this strategy --
1. Choose an appropriate format - Is your topic hierarchical?  A process?  Problem solving? Decision making? Compare/ contrast?  Read about different maps HERE. Copy and edit the map you want from the Google Drawing repository, AVID site, or Canva templates (see Resources).  You may also make your own using SmartDraw, Lucidchart, or Google Drawings
2. Create an “Answer key” - Organize the content info into your map to check that the spacing/# of boxes, etc.. is appropriate.      
3. Model how to use the map - Show students how to add info initially and then gradually release!  Or, start with interpreting/analyzing a completed map.
4. Reflect - Ask students - How did creating the map help you understand the concept?  Were there any surprises/challenges?
LEVEL UP - Put students in groups to create concepts maps on the vertical whiteboards!

Fan-N-Pick is a Kagan cooperative learning strategy that can be used in any subject. Students work in groups of four to review content using question cards.  Use it to practice collaboration skills (WICOR). Follow these steps-

1. Create Question Cards - based on the content you are teaching.  Questions should be open-ended to promote thinking and discussion.  Ask AI to help you create them! 
2. Group and # Students - 4 per group.
3. Student 1 - holds the question cards in a fan and says, “Pick a card, any card!”
4. Student 2 - picks a card, reads the question aloud and allows 5 seconds of “think time”.
5. Student 3 - answers the question.
6. Student 4 - paraphrases and praises or coaches to the corret answer.
7. Students rotate the roles and repeat the process for each new round.  This activity ensures equal participation, builds communication skills, and reinforces learning through questioning and peer support.

Description:
A cooperative learning technique that creates student-centered learning.
1. Create diverse groups of 5-6 students 
2. One person per group is appointed as leader
3. Divide the lesson into 5-6 segments
4. Assign each student in the group one segment to learn
5. Give student time to read their segment 2 or 3 times
6. Form expert groups by having one student from the original jigsaw group  join others with the same topic - they should discuss the main points of their segment and rehearse presentations for the main group
7. Initial group comes back together and each student presents their portion to the group
8. Teacher as a facilitator, goes from group to group to observe the process
9. Give a quiz on the material, this helps the students to take responsibility
Resource Links: Video: The Jigsaw Method

Description:
Set up designated areas where students engage in diverse tasks to reinforce, explore, or apply concepts. They promote active learning, collaboration, and differentiation by allowing students to interact with content in multiple ways.
- Identify Objectives: Define the skills or concepts each station will target.
- Plan Activities: Design varied tasks for skill reinforcement, collaboration, and application.
- Organize Materials: Prepare labeled resources and tools for each station.
- Provide Clear Instructions: Use written or visual directions for independent work.
- Group Strategically: Form groups based on skill level or collaboration needs.
- Set Time Limits: Establish a rotation schedule with clear timing.
- Differentiate Tasks: Adjust task complexity or offer choices for diverse learners.
- Facilitate and Monitor: Circulate to support and assess understanding.
- Debrief: Conclude with reflection or an exit ticket for assessment.

Description:
This oldie but goodie Kagan strategy is great for developing collaboration skills (WICOR). Follow these steps to make it happen - 
1. Group and # Students - 3-4 per group.
2. Explain the strategy - Students will get time to think and write an answer to a question. Then they share their answers in their group and arrive at consensus.  Finally, a student in each group shares their answer
3. Pose the Question/Problem - open-ended, compelling questions work best.
4. Think & Write - students individually think and write their answer (on paper or boards)
5. Heads Together - Students share their thinking in groups and come to a consensus
6. Share Out - Pick a # (ie. “#3s share your answer”). That student shares their group’s answer.  Other group members can support them if they forget or misspeak 
7. Praise, Push, and Clarify - Praise student responses, push their thinking with follow-up questions, and clarify misconceptions

Free paint chips (aka swatches, color samples) can be used for creative writing projects.  Here’s how it works . . . 
1. Get paint chips from the hardware store - optional: holepunch and make color sets with binder rings.
2. Students categorize the chips in their groups into setting (ie “Cactus Bloom”), characters (ie “Purple Phantom”), magical objects (ie “Sunken Treasure”), plot twists, pets, villains, problems/solutions, etc... 
3. Students write a short story with a chip from each category or a quick write, poem, imagery analysis, etc.. from one category.
- Science - write a fictional story about a process (photosynthesis, meiosis). Colors are characters  
- Social Studies - pick a color(s) to represent a historical event and explain why (ie. grays - Industrial Revolution)
- Math - Calculate the probability of randomly selecting a color or set of colors (ie primary)
Students can also make their own chips. Check out this blog post for more info!
Resource Links: Paint Chip Prompts: Student Writing Activities from the Hardware Aisle

- Should everyone go to college?
- Is it my job or the teacher’s job to motivate me?

- What’s more important -- practice or performance?

Skill(s): Collaboration (WICOR), Communication, Critical Thinking, SEL (Social emotional learning)
Description:
Two students partner up and take turns orally responding to a prompt. Responses are typically short, fast-paced, and idea-based, making it ideal for brainstorming, reviewing content, or activating prior knowledge. Follow these steps - 
1.  Create Partners - Person A & Person B
2. Give a Clear Prompt - “Identify the causes 
     of the French Revolution.” “Show each step 
     to solve the equation.” “List ideas to get  
     students through the lunch line quicker.”
3. Give Clear Instructions - Students will take 
     turns contributing one idea/step at a time.
4. Set a Timer - Show a visible timer of 30-90 
    seconds for each partner to share.
5. Circulate, Listen, Coach - Help struggling pairs with hints. Praise/Redirect as needed.
6. Debrief or Share Out - Each pair shares one strong idea. “Share an idea your partner contributed.” “Share one thing you learned.”

Variations
- Students think about and write responses before the timer starts.  
- If a student is stuck, they can ask their partner for a hint or to clarify their response.
- Give students a list of sentence starters
Resource Links: Rally Robin Kagan Structure One-Pager

Reflection Sentence Stems - Provide sentence starters to scaffold reflection
        - “One strategy that helped me learn was…”

     4. Exit Ticket Goal Checks - Use exit tickets once or twice weekly focused on progress.

       - One habit I used well today was…
       - One habit I need to improve is…"

Description:
6 Ways to Scaffold Learning
How can you get students from “confusion to clarity?”
1. Clarity - Make instructions brief and ultra-clear.  Simplify, simplify, simplify with headings and annotations directed to key points.
2. Fill in Background Knowledge - Help students learn key terms through word walls, vocab games, KWL activities to connect what they know to new content.  
3. Multiple Representations - Provide students opportunities to interact with the content in written, picture, diagram, video, ... form.
4. Graphic Organizers/Anchor Charts - Boost comprehension/retention with a thoughtful visual scaffold that highlights key ideas/ concepts.
5. Pre-Lesson Activities - A practice quiz BEFORE the learning helps students organize thoughts, spark curiosity, and encourage educated guesses as to the right answer.
6. Ask Metacognitive ?s -  Help students become more independent by asking them: What stands out? What parts are unfamiliar?, What ?s do I have?
Resource Links: 6 Foundational Ways to Scaffold Student Learning

A creative way to take notes using words, visuals, and symbols turning note-taking into a visual and memorable experience.
- Start with a blank page.
- Use simple icons and drawings to represent key ideas.
- Combine headings, bullet points, arrows, and borders to organize information.
- Highlight main concepts with bold text, colors, or shapes.
- Use hierarchy by making important words larger or in bold.
- Add connectors; arrows or lines to show relationships
- Keep it simple and clear - it is about understanding, not perfection.
- Leave space for adding details later.
- Use symbols (lightbulb = ideas, star = important, question mark = unclear).
- Review and refine after the lesson to complete missing parts.
Use Sketchnote during lectures, while reading, for review, as a processing tool, for presentations, or as a reflection
Sketchnote increases focus and engagement, helps with memory retention, supports visual learners, allows for creativity, and encourages critical thinking by prioritizing key ideas

Description:
Snorkl is an AI-powered tool that lets students explain their thinking through voice recordings, drawings, or text. AI gives instant feedback on their reasoning, while teachers get a dashboard that highlights student understanding, misconceptions, and growth over time.

Use it for . . .
- Think-Alouds: Students record explanations or show work on a digital whiteboard.
- Formative Checks: Snorkl identifies patterns and misconceptions in student responses.
- Differentiation: Students choose voice, text, or drawing; AI feedback adapts to their level.
- Peer Learning: Students can view anonymous examples to learn from strong explanations.

Open Snorkl and sign in with Google, create classes, assign prompts or activities, and students join your class with a code.  Choose a ready-made activity or create your own.

App Name: The STRIDE Method for Word Problems
Description:
Help students organize their thinking with the STRIDE Method –-
S - Survey the Problem - Read the entire problem to understand the story/context. Identify (circle/underline) all important info.  What is it asking?
T - Think About Connections  - Connect the problem to what you already know. Visualize/Draw and label the situation.
R - Reflect on the Questions - What specifically is the problem asking me to find?  What will the answer look like? Rephrase the ?
I - Identify a Strategy - What steps, operations, or procedures will help solve the problem?
D - Do the Work - Carry out your strategy.  Show your work clearly with reasoning to track your thinking process and review later.
E - Evaluate Your Answer - Check your final answer.  Does it make sense in the context of the situation?  If it does not make sense, revisit your understanding and/or strategy.
Resource Links: Tackle Word Problems in STRIDE

Description:
Students actively learn through peer interviews. It reinforces lesson content, promotes movement, engagement, and deeper content understanding.
Divide the Class into 2 Groups (A & B)
Provide Questions:
- Give Group A two questions from the lesson.
- Give Group B two different questions.
- Have each student create one original question based on the material.
Partner Up:
- Group A student partners with a Group B student.
- Partners ask and answer their first question (1 minute each), then switch partners to answer the second question.
Class Discussion:
- After each question is answered, students return to their seats.
- Review answers and ask students to share their original questions.
Reflection:
- Use student responses to assess understanding and clarify key concepts.
Resource Links: Video: Three Question Interview - Classroom Strategy

Description:
In small groups (3-4), students respond to a prompt on a shared Google slide (see template in resources) and then do a short presentation to teach the class what they have learned - similar to a jigsaw.

Sample prompts for different groups for . . .Science
- Describe the first phase of photosynthesis. 
- Describe the second phase of photosynthesis?
- What parts of the cell are involved in photosynthesis?
- What is ATP?  Explain the parts.

Sample prompts for different groups for . . .Social Studies 
- List some reasons why countries go to war.  Explain each one.
- What was the political and economic situation in Germany before WWII?
- What sparked WWII and why?
- Describe the alliances formed between countries in WWII.
- Describe the weaponry and tactics used in WWII.

Description:
Updating Notes takes minimal preparation and it is a great way to use the 
Here’s how to do it:
- Lecture for 10 minutes
- Give students 2 minutes to share and compare their notes with a partner (collaboration).
- Give students 2 minutes to make revisions in their notes.

This technique allows students to take a break from listening, reflect, interact with peers, fill in gaps, develop joint questions as well as encouraging them to create better notes.

Description:
Practicing gratitude has powerful benefits for students including better mental health, greater resilience, stronger social connections, stress reduction, and a more positive mindset about the future.
A Gratitude Journal is a personal reflection (ungraded) with brief entries and optional sharing with peers or the whole class.
This article lists gratitude prompts for 15 days, but this can be shortened as needed.  Each week has a theme . . .
- Week 1: Reflecting on Our School Year\
- Week 2: Appreciating Our Present
- Week 3: Looking Forward with Gratitude
It also has activity ideas such as a gratitude circle, gratitude wall, thank-you letter writing, gratitude quotes, and even planting a gratitude garden. 
Students can continue this journal over the summer to benefit and grow even more
Resource Links: End the Year with Gratitude: A 15-Day Journal Practice for Students
Resources: [End the Year with Gratitude: A 15-Day Journal Practice for Students](https://blog.tcea.org/end-school-year-with-gratitude-15-day-journal-practice-for-students/)

Description:
Today’s students crave constant stimulation and entertainment. Ideas to draw them in include -  Read the article for examples.
1. Surprise Them - Add novelty or something unusual/unexpected to arouse curiosity 😲.  Could be a song, odd clothes/accessory.
2. Present Odd Facts, Anomalies - Give students info that contradicts their intuition or a live demo that defies logic 🤯.
3. Invite Student Predictions - Students guess what will happen and eagerly anticipate the “Big Reveal” 😅.
4. Pose a Provocative (Hook) ? - Seek out a burning, open-ended question related to your lesson content. Students will 🤔.
5. Cite a Current Event/Issue - Raise awareness of breaking news 📰 relevant to them. Ask for their reaction/opinion.
6. Be Humorous - Use a funny joke 😂, meme, cartoon, comic strip, song, or video to give students a shot of dopamine and segue into your lesson with momentum.
